# Voice Guide -- [domain]

<!-- Generated by Practitioner SEO onboarding voice analysis.
     Review and edit before use. The writer skill reads this file before
     producing any draft. -->


## Tone register

[Describe the overall tone. Is it authoritative, conversational, academic,
casual, or a specific mix? How does the writer balance confidence with
approachability?]


## Sentence patterns

[Describe sentence length and rhythm. What is the average length? What is the
range? Does the writer favor short punchy sentences, complex compound
structures, or a deliberate mix? What is the rhythm -- does every sentence
have a similar cadence, or is there intentional variation?]


## Paragraph patterns

[Describe paragraph structure. How many sentences per paragraph? Does the
writer lead with the claim (deductive) or build to it (inductive)? Are
single-sentence paragraphs used for emphasis or as a default? How does
paragraph length vary between intros, body sections, and conclusions?]


## POV and person

[First person, third person, or mixed? "I" vs. "we"? How consistently is the
chosen POV maintained? Does it shift between page types (e.g., first person
in guides, third person in news)?]


## Authority signals

[How does the writer deploy their credentials? Sparingly and only when it
adds information, or frequently as a framing device? Does the writer use the
professional aside ("In my experience...", "When I build/teach/coach...")?
Are credentials specific (names the project, the year, the outcome) or
general ("I've been doing this for years")?]


## Evidence patterns

[Does the writer cite specific sources, dates, numbers, and measurements?
Or make general claims? How dense is the factual specificity? Does the
writer name products, tools, techniques by their proper names?]


## What NOT to do

[Anti-patterns the writer avoids. These become forbidden-word candidates.
Examples might include: never uses superlatives, never hedges, never opens
with a rhetorical question, never addresses the reader as "you" in certain
contexts, avoids certain phrases or constructions.]
